mission.title: Native JSON Authoring Validation (L2) — the spacing effect / distributed practice
mission.decision_supported: Whether NH should recommend spaced study/practice in an internal learning-and-onboarding note.
research_question: Does spacing study over time improve long-term retention versus massing, and how far does the evidence generalize?
mission.scope: Human learning/memory research ~1885-2024; primarily verbal-recall and lab studies + reviews; public sources.
mission.out_of_scope: Clinical memory disorders; animal studies; whole-classroom policy design.
mission.reviewer: Founder (author-reviewer; L2 — independent reviewer preferred, not required, per NH-ROP §18)

claims.statement: Distributing study over time (spacing) improves long-term retention compared with massing the same study time (the spacing effect).
claims.confidence_rationale: Large meta-analytic base (Cepeda 2006) plus an independent high-utility rating (Dunlosky 2013); kept at Supported (not Confirmed) because primary access here was secondary.
claims.overturn_conditions: A credible meta-analysis showing no or reversed spacing benefit.
claims.origin: Initial anchor hypothesis, tested against Cepeda 2006 + Dunlosky 2013.

claims.statement: Cepeda et al. (2006) pooled about 254 studies (>14,000 participants) and found distributed practice reliably outperformed massed practice, with a large rather than marginal advantage.
claims.scope: The Cepeda et al. 2006 meta-analysis of verbal-recall studies.
claims.confidence_rationale: Consistently reported meta-analysis scope/result; reached via secondary summaries, not the primary article.
claims.overturn_conditions: A corrected reading of the meta-analysis materially changing scope or effect.

claims.statement: The optimal inter-study gap grows with the desired retention interval (about 20% of the test delay at a few weeks, falling to about 5% at one year).
claims.scope: Verbal-recall tasks; Cepeda et al. 2008 modelling.
claims.confidence_rationale: Reported quantitative relationship from a single lab's modelling study; not independently replicated across labs here.
claims.overturn_conditions: Independent work materially contradicting the gap/retention-interval relationship.

claims.statement: The spacing effect generalizes equally to all materials, including complex real-world skills and authentic classroom learning.
claims.confidence_rationale: Robust across many verbal/lab tasks, but most evidence is verbal recall in the lab; complex-skill and authentic-classroom transfer is less established.
claims.overturn_conditions: Broad evidence of comparable spacing benefits for complex skills and authentic classroom outcomes.
claims.origin: Breadth of lab evidence vs transfer limitation.

claims.statement: Spaced repetition is implemented in widely-used learning software via flashcard scheduling algorithms (e.g., Leitner / SM-2).
claims.confidence_rationale: Documented in the software's own materials; establishes implementation, not learning-outcome magnitude.
claims.overturn_conditions: Evidence that such scheduling is not actually spacing-based.

claims.statement: Rigorous evidence of spacing benefits in authentic classroom/professional settings is more limited than the laboratory verbal-recall evidence base.
claims.confidence_rationale: Within the reviewed literature, most evidence is lab/verbal; authentic-setting and complex-skill studies are fewer. This does not prove none exist.
claims.overturn_conditions: A substantial body of authentic-setting controlled studies.

claims.statement: Spacing improves conceptual understanding and transfer, not merely verbatim recall.
claims.confidence_rationale: The strongest evidence concerns recall/retention; transfer-and-understanding effects are less consistently measured in the reviewed scope.
claims.overturn_conditions: Consistent controlled evidence of spacing benefits on transfer/understanding measures.
claims.origin: Recall-focused evidence vs the transfer question.

evidence.exact_finding: Meta-analysis: distributed practice reliably beat massed practice; advantage large, not marginal.
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: distributed practice reliably beat massed; large advantage
evidence.location: Cepeda, Pashler, Vul, Wixted & Rohrer (2006), Psychological Bulletin
evidence.researcher_notes: MD trace: RESEARCH_RECORD.md observation O1 / M007-C1-E1

evidence.exact_finding: An independent review rated distributed practice as a high-utility learning technique.
evidence.location: Dunlosky et al. (2013), Psychological Science in the Public Interest

evidence.exact_finding: The meta-analysis pooled ~254 studies and >14,000 participants.
evidence.limitations: secondary access; some summaries cite 317 experiments / 184 articles

evidence.exact_finding: Optimal inter-study interval was ~20% of the test delay at a few weeks, falling to ~5% at one year.
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: optimal gap ~20% (weeks) -> ~5% (one year) of retention interval

evidence.exact_finding: The spacing benefit held across many verbal-learning tasks and conditions.
evidence.limitations: breadth within verbal/lab domain, not all materials

evidence.exact_finding: Most spacing evidence is verbal recall in the lab; generalization to complex skills and authentic classrooms is less established.
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: lab/verbal skew; transfer to complex/authentic less established
evidence.limitations: a scope caveat, not a counter-measurement

evidence.exact_finding: Spaced-repetition flashcard software schedules reviews using Leitner/SM-2-style algorithms.
Authentic classroom/professional controlled studies of spacing are fewer than the lab verbal-recall base.

sources.title: Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis
sources.reliability_notes: large meta-analysis; the synthesis aggregates many independent studies

sources.title: Spacing effects in learning: a temporal ridgeline of optimal retention
sources.reliability_notes: same research group as S001 (shared lineage L01)

sources.title: Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques
sources.publisher: Psychological Science in the Public Interest
sources.reliability_notes: independent review team; rated distributed practice high-utility

sources.title: Learning-science reviews on the generalization/limits of the spacing effect
sources.url_or_internal_location: review commentary on spacing generalization (lab vs authentic)
sources.reliability_notes: scope/limitation caveats, partly noted within the reviews themselves

sources.title: Spaced-repetition flashcard software documentation (Leitner / SM-2 scheduling)
sources.reliability_notes: documents implementation; not an outcome study

assumptions.statement: Lab verbal-recall spacing findings generalize to authentic classroom/professional learning and complex skills.
assumptions.why_needed: to move from lab evidence to a workplace-learning recommendation.

unknowns.exact_open_question: How large is the spacing benefit for complex-skill transfer and multi-year retention in authentic settings?
unknowns.why_it_matters: the universality (C4) and transfer (C7) claims hinge on this.

contradictions.nature_of_conflict: Broad lab robustness of the spacing effect vs the more limited authentic-setting/complex-skill evidence.
contradictions.scope_differences: verbal/lab tasks vs authentic classroom/complex skills
contradictions.impact_on_verdict: keeps C4 Partially Supported and supports C6.

verdicts.confidence_rationale: Large meta-analysis + independent high-utility review; secondary access.
Single-lab modelling; not independently replicated here.
Robust in verbal/lab; complex/authentic transfer less established.
Documented implementation; not an outcome study.
Reviewed literature skews lab/verbal; not proof none exist.
verdicts.overturn_conditions: a body of authentic controlled studies.

verdicts.confidence_rationale: Strongest evidence is recall; transfer/understanding less consistently measured.
